entries.body:
More general systems having the form
\[
\mathbf{B}_0\mathbf{u}_t + \sum_{j=1}^n \mathbf{B}_j\mathbf{u}_{x_j} = \mathbf{f}
\tag{9}
\]
are also called \textit{symmetric}, provided the matrices $\mathbf{B}_j$ are symmetric for $j = 0,\ldots,n$. The theory set forth below easily extends to such systems, provided $\mathbf{B}_0$ is positive definite.

Symmetric hyperbolic systems of the type (9) generalize the second-order hyperbolic PDE studied above. For suppose $v$ is a smooth solution of the scalar equation
\[
v_{tt} - \sum_{i,j=1}^n a^{ij}v_{x_ix_j} = 0,
\tag{10}
\]
where without loss we may take $a^{ij} = a^{ji}$ $(i,j = 1,\ldots,n)$. Writing
\[
\mathbf{u} = (u^1,\ldots,u^{n+1}) := (v_{x_1},\ldots,v_{x_n},v_t),
\]
we discover $\mathbf{u}$ solves a system of the form (9), for $m = n+1$, $\mathbf{f} \equiv 0$,
\[
\mathbf{B}_j = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & \cdots & 0 & -a^{1j} \\ & \ddots & & \vdots \\ 0 & \cdots & 0 & -a^{nj} \\ -a^{1j} & \cdots & -a^{nj} & 0 \end{pmatrix}_{(n+1)\times(n+1)} \quad (j = 1,\ldots,n),
\]
\[
\mathbf{B}_0 = \begin{pmatrix} a^{11} & \cdots & a^{1n} & 0 \\ & \ddots & & \vdots \\ a^{n1} & \cdots & a^{nn} & 0 \\ 0 & \cdots & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}_{(n+1)\times(n+1)}.
\]
Observe that the uniform hyperbolicity condition for (10) implies that the matrix $\mathbf{B}_0$ is positive definite.

entries.label: def:weak-solution-symmetric-hyperbolic-system
entries.title: Weak solution of a symmetric hyperbolic system
entries.body:
We say
\[
\mathbf{u} \in L^2(0,T; H^1(\mathbb{R}^n; \mathbb{R}^m)), \text{ with } \mathbf{u}' \in L^2(0,T; L^2(\mathbb{R}^n; \mathbb{R}^m)),
\]
is a weak solution of the initial-value problem (5) for the symmetric hyperbolic system provided

(i) $(\mathbf{u}',\mathbf{v}) + B[\mathbf{u},\mathbf{v};t] = (\mathbf{f},\mathbf{v})$

for each $\mathbf{v} \in H^1(\mathbb{R}^n; \mathbb{R}^m)$ and a.e. $0 \le t \le T$, and

(ii) $\mathbf{u}(0) = \mathbf{g}$.

Here and afterwards $(\cdot, \cdot)$ denotes the inner product in $L^2(\mathbb{R}^n; \mathbb{R}^m)$.

deps.title: Symmetric systems generalize second-order hyperbolic PDE
deps.label: rem:symmetric-hyperbolic-general-systems

entries.label: rem:weak-solution-symmetric-hyperbolic-continuity
entries.title: Continuity of the weak solution
entries.body: According to Theorem 2 in \S5.9, $\mathbf{u} \in C([0,T]; L^2(\mathbb{R}^n; \mathbb{R}^m))$ and so the initial condition (ii) makes sense.

deps.title: Weak solution of a symmetric hyperbolic system
deps.label: def:weak-solution-symmetric-hyperbolic-system

entries.label: thm:symmetric-hyperbolic-approximate-solutions
entries.title: Existence of approximate solutions
entries.body:
For each $\epsilon > 0$, there exists a unique solution $\mathbf{u}^\epsilon$ of (11), with
\[
\mathbf{u}^\epsilon \in L^2(0,T; H^3(\mathbb{R}^n; \mathbb{R}^m)), \quad (\mathbf{u}^\epsilon)' \in L^2(0,T; H^1(\mathbb{R}^n; \mathbb{R}^m)).
\tag{12}
\]

deps.title: Fundamental solution of the heat equation
deps.label: def:fundamental-solution-heat-equation

entries.label: thm:symmetric-hyperbolic-energy-estimates
entries.title: Energy estimates
entries.body:
There exists a constant $C$, depending only on $n$ and the coefficients, such that
\[
\max_{0 \leq t \leq T} \|u^\epsilon(t)\|_{H^1(\mathbb{R}^n;\mathbb{R}^m)} + \|u_t^\epsilon\|_{L^2(0,T;L^2(\mathbb{R}^n;\mathbb{R}^m))}
\]
\[
\leq C(\|g\|_{H^1(\mathbb{R}^n;\mathbb{R}^m)} + \|f\|_{L^2(0,T;H^1(\mathbb{R}^n;\mathbb{R}^m))} + \|f'\|_{L^2(0,T;L^2(\mathbb{R}^n;\mathbb{R}^m))})
\tag{17}
\]
for each $0 < \epsilon \leq 1$.

entries.label: thm:symmetric-hyperbolic-existence-weak-solution
entries.title: Existence of weak solution
entries.body: There exists a weak solution of the initial value problem (5).

deps.title: Weak solution of a symmetric hyperbolic system
deps.label: def:weak-solution-symmetric-hyperbolic-system

entries.label: thm:symmetric-hyperbolic-uniqueness-weak-solution
entries.title: Uniqueness of weak solution
entries.body: A weak solution of (5) is unique.

deps.title: Weak solution of a symmetric hyperbolic system
deps.label: def:weak-solution-symmetric-hyperbolic-system

entries.label: thm:constant-coefficient-hyperbolic-existence
entries.title: Existence of solution
entries.body:
Assume
\[
\mathbf{g} \in H^s(\mathbb{R}^n; \mathbb{R}^m) \quad \left(s > \frac{n}{2} + m\right).
\]
Then there is a unique solution $\mathbf{u} \in C^1([0, \infty); \mathbb{R}^m)$ of the initial-value problem (32), (33).

entries.label: def:semigroup-contraction-semigroup
entries.title: Semigroup, contraction semigroup
entries.body:
(i) A family $\{S(t)\}_{t \geq 0}$ of bounded linear operators mapping $X$ into $X$ is called a semigroup if conditions (4)--(6) are satisfied.

(ii) We say $\{S(t)\}_{t \geq 0}$ is a contraction semigroup if in addition
\[
\|S(t)\| \leq 1 \quad (t \geq 0),
\tag{7}
\]
$\|\cdot\|$ here denoting the operator norm. Thus
\[
\|S(t)u\| \leq \|u\| \quad (t \geq 0, u \in X).
\]

entries.label: def:semigroup-generator
entries.title: Infinitesimal generator
entries.body:
Write
\[
D(A) := \left\{u \in X \mid \lim_{t \to 0+} \frac{S(t)u - u}{t} \text{ exists in } X\right\}
\tag{8}
\]
and
\[
Au := \lim_{t \to 0+} \frac{S(t)u - u}{t} \quad (u \in D(A)).
\tag{9}
\]
We call $A : D(A) \to X$ the (infinitesimal) generator of the semigroup $\{S(t)\}_{t \geq 0}$; $D(A)$ is the domain of $A$.

entries.label: thm:semigroup-differential-properties
entries.title: Differential properties of semigroups
entries.body:
Assume $u \in D(A)$. Then
(i) $S(t)u \in D(A)$ for each $t \geq 0$,
(ii) $AS(t)u = S(t)Au$ for each $t > 0$,
(iii) the mapping $t \mapsto S(t)u$ is differentiable for each $t > 0$,
and
(iv) $\frac{d}{dt}S(t)u = AS(t)u$ \quad $(t > 0)$.

entries.label: rem:semigroup-c1-remark
entries.title: Continuous differentiability of the flow
entries.body: Since $t \mapsto AS(t)u = S(t)Au$ is continuous, the mapping $t \mapsto S(t)u$ is $C^1$ in $(0, \infty)$, if $u \in D(A)$.

entries.label: thm:semigroup-generator-properties
entries.title: Properties of generators
entries.body:
(i) The domain $D(A)$ is dense in $X$,

and

(ii) $A$ is a closed operator.

entries.label: rem:closed-operator-definition
entries.title: Meaning of ``closed operator''
entries.body:
To say $A$ is closed means that whenever $u_k \in D(A)$ $(k = 1, \ldots)$ and $u_k \to u$, $Au_k \to v$ as $k \to \infty$, then
\[
u \in D(A), \quad v = Au.
\]

entries.label: def:resolvent-set-operator
entries.title: Resolvent set and resolvent operator
entries.body:
(i) We say a real number $\lambda$ belongs to $\rho(A)$, the resolvent set of $A$, provided the operator
\[
\lambda I - A : D(A) \to X
\]
is one-to-one and onto.

(ii) If $\lambda \in \rho(A)$, the resolvent operator $R_\lambda : X \to X$ is defined by
\[
R_\lambda u := (\lambda I - A)^{-1}u.
\]

entries.label: thm:resolvent-operator-properties
entries.title: Properties of resolvent operators
entries.body:
(i) If $\lambda, \mu \in \rho(A)$, we have
\[
R_\lambda - R_\mu = (\mu - \lambda)R_\lambda R_\mu \quad \text{(resolvent identity)}
\tag{12}
\]
and
\[
R_\lambda R_\mu = R_\mu R_\lambda.
\tag{13}
\]

(ii) If $\lambda > 0$, then $\lambda \in \rho(A)$,
\[
R_\lambda u = \int_0^\infty e^{-\lambda t}S(t)u \, dt \quad (u \in X),
\tag{14}
\]
and so $\|R_\lambda\| \leq \frac{1}{\lambda}$.

entries.label: rem:resolvent-laplace-transform-semigroup
entries.title: Resolvent as Laplace transform
entries.body: Thus the resolvent operator is the Laplace transform of the semigroup (cf. \cref{ex:resolvent-laplace-transform}).

entries.label: thm:hille-yosida-theorem
entries.title: Hille--Yosida Theorem
entries.body:
Let $A$ be a closed, densely-defined linear operator on $X$. Then $A$ is the generator of a contraction semigroup $\{S(t)\}_{t \geq 0}$ if and only if
\[
(0,\infty) \subset \rho(A) \text{ and } \|R_\lambda\| \leq \frac{1}{\lambda} \text{ for } \lambda > 0.
\tag{17}
\]

entries.label: rem:omega-contractive-semigroup
entries.title: $\omega$-contractive semigroups
entries.body:
Let $\omega \in \mathbb{R}$. A semigroup $\{S(t)\}_{t \ge 0}$ is called \textit{$\omega$-contractive} if $\|S(t)\| \le e^{\omega t}$ $(t \ge 0)$. An easy variant of \cref{thm:hille-yosida-theorem} asserts that a closed, densely-defined linear operator $A$ generates an $\omega$-contractive semigroup if and only if
\[
(\omega, \infty) \subset \rho(A) \text{ and } \|R_\lambda\| \le \frac{1}{\lambda - \omega} \text{ for all } \lambda > \omega.
\tag{23}
\]
This version of the Hille--Yosida Theorem will be required for our first example below.

entries.label: thm:parabolic-pde-as-semigroup
entries.title: Second-order parabolic PDE as semigroups
entries.body: The operator $A$ generates a $\gamma$-contraction semigroup $\{S(t)\}_{t \ge 0}$ on $L^2(U)$.

deps.title: First Existence Theorem for weak solutions
deps.label: thm:first-existence-weak-solutions

entries.label: rem:parabolic-semigroup-remark
entries.title: Comparison with the Galerkin method
entries.body: Semigroup theory provides an elegant method for constructing a solution to the initial/boundary-value problem (24). It is worth noting however that this technique requires that the coefficients $a^{ij}, b^i, c$ ($i, j = 1, \ldots, n$) of $L$ be independent of $t$. The Galerkin method of \cref{sec:second-order-parabolic-equations} works without this restriction. On the other hand, semigroup theory constructs at the outset a more regular solution than that produced by the Galerkin technique, at least until we develop regularity theory.

entries.label: thm:hyperbolic-pde-as-semigroup
entries.title: Second-order hyperbolic PDE as semigroups
entries.body: The operator $A$ generates a contraction semigroup $\{S(t)\}_{t \geq 0}$ on $H_0^1(U) \times L^2(U)$.

entries.label: thm:euler-lagrange-equation-pde-minimizer
entries.title: Euler--Lagrange equation for a minimizer
entries.body:
Any smooth minimizer $u$ of $I[\cdot]$, subject to the boundary condition $u = g$ on $\partial U$, is a solution of the Euler--Lagrange partial differential equation
\[
-\sum_{i=1}^{n}(L_{p_i}(Du, u, x))_{x_i} + L_z(Du, u, x) = 0 \quad \text{in } U.
\]
This is a quasilinear, second-order PDE in divergence form. Thus--conversely--we can try to find a solution of this equation by searching for minimizers of (4).

entries.label: ex:dirichlets-principle-calculus-of-variations
entries.title: Dirichlet's principle
entries.body:
Take
\[
L(p, z, x) = \frac{1}{2}|p|^2.
\]
Then $L_{p_i} = p_i$ ($i = 1, \ldots, n$), $L_z = 0$; and so the Euler--Lagrange equation associated with the functional
\[
I[w] := \frac{1}{2}\int_U |Dw|^2 \, dx
\]
is
\[
\Delta u = 0 \quad \text{in } U.
\]
This fact is \textit{Dirichlet's principle}, previously introduced in \S2.2.5 (see \cref{thm:dirichlets-principle}).

entries.label: ex:generalized-dirichlets-principle
entries.title: Generalized Dirichlet's principle
entries.body:
Write
\[
L(p, z, x) = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i,j=1}^{n} a^{ij}(x) p_i p_j - zf(x),
\]
where $a^{ij} = a^{ji}$ ($i, j = 1, \ldots, n$). Then $L_{p_i} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} a^{ij}(x) p_j$ ($i = 1, \ldots, n$), $L_z = -f(x)$. Hence the Euler--Lagrange equation associated with the functional
\[
I[w] := \int_U \left\{\frac{1}{2}\sum_{i,j=1}^{n} a^{ij} w_{x_i} w_{x_j} - wf\right\} dx
\]
is the divergence structure linear equation
\[
-\sum_{i,j=1}^{n}(a^{ij} u_{x_j})_{x_i} = f \quad \text{in } U.
\]
We will see later (in \cref{sec:basic-ideas-calculus-of-variations,sec:existence-of-minimizers}) that the uniform ellipticity condition on the $a^{ij}$ $(i,j = 1, \ldots, n)$ is a natural further assumption, required to prove the existence of a minimizer. Consequently from the nonlinear viewpoint of the calculus of variations, the divergence structure form of a linear second-order elliptic PDE is completely natural. This observation provides some much belated motivation for the bilinear form techniques utilized in \cref{chap:second-order-elliptic-equations}.

entries.label: ex:nonlinear-poisson-equation-calculus-of-variations
entries.title: Nonlinear Poisson equation
entries.body:
Given a smooth function $f : \R \to \R$, define its antiderivative $F(z) = \int_0^z f(y)\, dy$. Then the Euler--Lagrange equation associated with the functional
\[
I[w] := \int_U \frac{1}{2}|Dw|^2 - F(w)\, dx
\]
is the nonlinear Poisson equation
\[
-\Delta u = f(u) \text{ in } U.
\]

entries.label: ex:minimal-surfaces-calculus-of-variations
entries.title: Minimal surfaces
entries.body:
Let
\[
L(p, z, x) = (1 + |p|^2)^{1/2};
\]
so that
\[
I[w] = \int_U (1 + |Dw|^2)^{1/2} \, dx
\]
is the area of the graph of the function $w : U \to \R$. The associated Euler--Lagrange equation is
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( \frac{u_{x_i}}{(1 + |Du|^2)^{1/2}} \right)_{x_i} = 0 \text{ in } U.
\tag{10}
\]
This partial differential equation is the \textit{minimal surface equation}. The expression $\Div\left(\frac{Du}{(1+|Du|^2)^{1/2}}\right)$ on the left side of (10) is $n$ times the \textit{mean curvature} of the graph of $u$. Thus a \textit{minimal surface} has zero mean curvature.

entries.label: prop:second-variation-convexity-necessary-condition
entries.title: Second variation necessary condition
entries.body:
If $u$ is a smooth minimizer of $I[\cdot]$ as in \cref{thm:euler-lagrange-equation-pde-minimizer}, then
\[
\sum_{i,j=1}^n L_{p_i p_j}(Du, u, x)\xi_i \xi_j \geq 0 \quad (\xi \in \R^n, x \in U).
\tag{14}
\]

entries.label: thm:euler-lagrange-system-pde-minimizer
entries.title: Euler--Lagrange system for a minimizer
entries.body:
Any smooth minimizer $\mathbf{u} = (u^1,\ldots,u^m)$ of $I[\cdot]$ defined by (15), taken among functions equal to $\mathbf{g}$ on $\partial U$, solves the coupled, quasilinear \textit{system} of PDE
\[
-\sum_{i=1}^n \left( L_{p_i^k}(D\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{u}, x) \right)_{x_i} + L_{z^k}(D\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{u}, x) = 0 \quad \text{in } U \quad (k = 1, \ldots, m).
\]
These are the \textit{Euler--Lagrange equations} for the functional $I[\cdot]$ defined by (15).

entries.label: def:null-lagrangian
entries.title: Null Lagrangian
entries.body:
The function $L$ is called a \textit{null Lagrangian} if the system of Euler--Lagrange equations
\[
-\sum_{i=1}^{n} (L_{p_i^k}(D\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{u}, x))_{x_i} + L_{z_k}(D\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{u}, x) = 0 \quad \text{in } U \quad (k = 1, \ldots, m)
\tag{17}
\]
is automatically solved by all smooth functions $\mathbf{u} : U \to \R^m$.

entries.label: thm:null-lagrangians-boundary-conditions
entries.title: Null Lagrangians and boundary conditions
entries.body:
Let $L$ be a null Lagrangian. Assume $\mathbf{u}, \bar{\mathbf{u}}$ are two functions in $C^2(U, \R^m)$ such that
\[
\mathbf{u} = \bar{\mathbf{u}} \quad \text{on } \partial U .
\tag{18}
\]
Then
\[
I[\mathbf{u}] = I[\bar{\mathbf{u}}] .
\tag{19}
\]

entries.label: lem:cofactor-divergence-free
entries.title: Divergence-free rows of the cofactor matrix
entries.body:
Let $\mathbf{u} : \R^n \to \R^n$ be a smooth function. Then
\[
\sum_{i=1}^n (\cof D\mathbf{u})^k_{i,x_i} = 0 \quad (k = 1, \ldots, n).
\tag{20}
\]

entries.label: thm:determinant-null-lagrangian
entries.title: Determinants as null Lagrangians
entries.body:
The determinant function
\[
L(P) = \det P \quad (P \in \mathbb{M}^{n \times n})
\]
is a null Lagrangian.

deps.title: Divergence-free rows of the cofactor matrix
deps.label: lem:cofactor-divergence-free

entries.label: thm:brouwer-fixed-point-theorem
entries.title: Brouwer's Fixed Point Theorem
entries.body:
Assume
\[
\mathbf{u} : B(0,1) \to B(0,1)
\]
is continuous, where $B(0,1)$ denotes the closed unit ball in $\R^n$. Then $\mathbf{u}$ has a fixed point; that is, there exists a point $x \in B(0,1)$ with
\[
\mathbf{u}(x) = x.
\]

entries.label: def:coercivity-condition-lagrangian
entries.title: Coercivity condition

entries.label: def:admissible-class-sobolev-minimization
entries.title: Admissible class
entries.body:
We will henceforth write
\[
\mathcal{A} := \{w \in W^{1,q}(U) \mid w = g \text{ on } \partial U \text{ in the trace sense}\}
\]
to denote this class of \textit{admissible} functions $w$. Note in view of (4) that $I[w]$ is defined (but may equal $+\infty$) for each $w \in \mathcal{A}$.

entries.label: def:minimizing-sequence-calculus-of-variations
entries.title: Minimizing sequence
entries.body: We call a sequence $\{u_k\}_{k=1}^{\infty} \subset \mathcal{A}$ satisfying (7), i.e.\ $I[u_k]\to m := \inf_{w\in\mathcal{A}} I[w]$, a \textit{minimizing sequence}.

entries.label: def:weakly-lower-semicontinuous-functional
entries.title: Weakly lower semicontinuous functional
entries.body:
We say that a function $I[\cdot]$ is (sequentially) weakly lower semicontinuous on $W^{1,q}(U)$, provided
\[
I[u] \leq \liminf_{k \to \infty} I[u_k]
\]
whenever
\[
u_k \rightharpoonup u \text{ weakly in } W^{1,q}(U).
\]

entries.label: thm:weak-lower-semicontinuity-convex-lagrangian
entries.title: Weak lower semicontinuity
entries.body: Assume that $L$ is bounded below, and in addition the mapping $p \mapsto L(p,z,x)$ is convex, for each $z \in \R, x \in U$. Then $I[\cdot]$ is weakly lower semicontinuous on $W^{1,q}(U)$.

entries.label: rem:weak-lower-semicontinuity-proof-remark
entries.title: On the role of weak convergence in the proof
entries.body:
It is very important to understand how the foregoing proof deals with the weak convergence $Du_k \rightharpoonup Du$. The key is the convexity inequality (23), on the right hand side of which $Du_k$ appears linearly. Weak convergence is, by its very definition, compatible with linear expressions, and so the limit (25) holds. Remember that it is not in general true that $Du_k \to Du$ a.e., even if we pass to a subsequence.

The convergence of $u_k$ to $u$ in $L^q$ is much stronger, and so we do not need any convexity assumption concerning $z \mapsto L(p, z, x)$.

entries.label: thm:existence-minimizer-convex-coercive
entries.title: Existence of minimizer
entries.body:
Assume that $L$ satisfies the coercivity inequality (4) and is convex in the variable $p$. Suppose also the admissible set $\mathcal{A}$ is nonempty.

Then there exists at least one function $u \in \mathcal{A}$ solving
\[
I[u] = \min_{w \in \mathcal{A}} I[w] .
\]

entries.label: thm:uniqueness-minimizer-uniformly-convex
entries.title: Uniqueness of minimizer
entries.body: Suppose (29), (30) hold. Then a minimizer $u \in \mathcal{A}$ of $I[\cdot]$ is unique.

entries.label: def:weak-solution-euler-lagrange-scalar
entries.title: Weak solution of the Euler--Lagrange equation
entries.body:
We say $u \in \mathcal{A}$ is a \textit{weak solution} of the boundary-value problem (37) for the Euler--Lagrange equation provided
\[
\int_U \sum_{i=1}^n L_{p_i}(Du,u,x) v_{x_i} + L_z(Du,u,x) v \, dx = 0
\]
for all $v \in W_0^{1,q}(U)$.

entries.label: thm:weak-solution-euler-lagrange-scalar
entries.title: Solution of the Euler--Lagrange equation
entries.body:
Assume $L$ verifies the growth conditions (35), (36), and $u \in \mathcal{A}$ satisfies
\[
I[u] = \min_{w \in \mathcal{A}} I[w].
\]
Then $u$ is a weak solution of (37).

deps.title: Weak solution of the Euler--Lagrange equation
deps.label: def:weak-solution-euler-lagrange-scalar

entries.label: rem:weak-solution-minimizer-joint-convexity
entries.title: Weak solutions of the Euler--Lagrange equation need not be minimizers
entries.body:
In general, the Euler--Lagrange equation (37) will have other solutions which do not correspond to minima of $I[\cdot]$; see \cref{sec:critical-points-calculus-of-variations}. However, \textit{in the special case that the joint mapping $(p, z) \mapsto L(p, z, x)$ is convex for each $x$, then each weak solution is in fact a minimizer}.

To see this, suppose $u \in \mathcal{A}$ solves
\[
\begin{cases}
-\sum_{i=1}^n (L_{p_i}(Du, u, x))_{x_i} + L_z(Du, u, x) = 0 & \text{in } U \\
u = g & \text{on } \partial U
\end{cases}
\tag{42}
\]
in the weak sense and select any $w \in \mathcal{A}$. Utilizing the convexity of the mapping $(p, z) \mapsto L(p, z, x)$, we have
\[
L(p, z, x) + D_p L(p, z, x) \cdot (q - p) + D_z L(p, z, x) \cdot (w - z) \leq L(q, w, x).
\]
Let $p = Du(x)$, $q = Dw(x)$, $z = u(x)$, $w = w(x)$ and integrate over $U$:
\[
I[u] + \int_U D_p L(Du, u, x) \cdot (Dw - Du) + D_z L(Du, u, x)(w - u) \, dx \leq I[w].
\]
In view of (42) the second term on the right is zero, and therefore $I[u] \leq I[w]$ for each $w \in \mathcal{A}$.

entries.label: def:admissible-class-systems
entries.title: Admissible class for systems
entries.body:
Set
\[
\mathcal{A} = \{\mathbf{w} \in W^{1,q}(U; \R^m) \mid \mathbf{w} = \mathbf{g} \text{ on } \partial U \text{ in the trace sense}\},
\]
where $\mathbf{g} : \partial U \to \R^m$ is given.

entries.label: thm:existence-minimizer-systems
entries.title: Existence of minimizer, systems
entries.body:
Assume that $L$ satisfies the coercivity inequality (43) and is convex in the variable $P$. Suppose also the admissible set $\mathcal{A}$ is nonempty.

Then there exists $\mathbf{u} \in \mathcal{A}$ solving
\[
I[\mathbf{u}] = \min_{\mathbf{w} \in \mathcal{A}} I[\mathbf{w}].
\]

entries.label: thm:uniqueness-minimizer-systems
entries.title: Uniqueness of minimizer, systems
entries.body: Assume $L$ does not depend on $z$ and the mapping $P \mapsto L(P, x)$ is uniformly convex. Then a minimizer $\mathbf{u} \in \mathcal{A}$ of $I[\cdot]$ is unique.

entries.label: def:weak-solution-euler-lagrange-system
entries.title: Weak solution of the Euler--Lagrange system
entries.body:
We define $\mathbf{u} \in \mathcal{A}$ to be a \textit{weak solution} of (45) provided
\[
\sum_{k=1}^m \int_U \sum_{i=1}^n L_{p_k^i}(D\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{u}, x)w^k_{x_i} + L_{z^k}(D\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{u}, x)w^k \, dx = 0
\]
for all $\mathbf{w} \in W_0^{1,q}(U; \R^m)$, $\mathbf{w} = (w^1, \ldots, w^m)$.

entries.label: thm:weak-solution-euler-lagrange-system
entries.title: Solution of the Euler--Lagrange system
entries.body:
Assume $L$ verifies the growth conditions (44) and $\mathbf{u} \in \mathcal{A}$ satisfies
\[
I[\mathbf{u}] = \min_{\mathbf{w} \in \mathcal{A}} I[\mathbf{w}].
\]
Then $\mathbf{u}$ is a weak solution of (45).

deps.title: Weak solution of the Euler--Lagrange system
deps.label: def:weak-solution-euler-lagrange-system

entries.label: lem:weak-continuity-of-determinants
entries.title: Weak continuity of determinants
entries.body:
Assume $n < q < \infty$ and
\[
\mathbf{u}_k \rightharpoonup \mathbf{u} \quad \text{weakly in } W^{1,q}(U; \R^n).
\]
Then
\[
\det D\mathbf{u}_k \rightharpoonup \det D\mathbf{u} \quad \text{weakly in } L^{q/n}(U).
\]

deps.title: Divergence-free rows of the cofactor matrix
deps.label: lem:cofactor-divergence-free

entries.label: def:polyconvex-lagrangian
entries.title: Polyconvex Lagrangian
entries.body: A Lagrangian $L$ of the form (51) is called \textit{polyconvex} provided (52) holds.

entries.label: thm:lower-semicontinuity-polyconvex
entries.title: Lower semicontinuity of polyconvex functionals
entries.body: Suppose $n < q < \infty$. Assume also $L$ is bounded below and is polyconvex. Then $I[u]$ is weakly lower semicontinuous on $W^{1,q}(U;\R^n)$.

entries.label: thm:existence-minimizer-polyconvex
entries.title: Existence of minimizers, polyconvex functionals
entries.body:
Assume that $n < q < \infty$, and that $L$ satisfies the coercivity inequality (43) and is polyconvex. Suppose also the admissible set $\mathcal{A}$ is nonempty.

Then there exists $u \in \mathcal{A}$ solving
\[
I[u] = \min_{w \in \mathcal{A}} I[w].
\]

entries.label: ex:polyconvexity-nonlinear-elasticity
entries.title: Nonlinear elasticity and polyconvexity
entries.body:
We explain the interest in the hypothesis of polyconvexity with an application from nonlinear elasticity theory, where $n = 3$. We consider an elastic body, which initially has the reference configuration $U$. We then displace each point $x \in \partial U$ to a new position $\mathbf{g}(x)$ and wish to determine new displacement $\mathbf{u}(x)$ of each internal point $x \in U$.

If the material is \textit{hyperelastic}, there exists by definition an associated energy density $L$ such that the physical displacement $\mathbf{u}$ minimizes the internal energy functional
\[
I[\mathbf{w}] := \int_U L(D\mathbf{w}, x)\, dx
\]
over all admissible displacements $\mathbf{w} \in \mathcal{A}$. Now it seems reasonable physically that $L$, which represents the internal energy density from stretching and compression, may explicitly depend on the local change in volume, that is, on $\det D\mathbf{w}$. In other words, it is physically appropriate to suppose that $L$ has the form $L(P, x) = F(P, \det P, x)$. Then $F$ describes in its first argument changes in internal energy due to changes in line elements, and in its second argument changes in internal energy due to changes in volume elements. See Ball \cite{Ball1977} for more explanation.

entries.label: thm:second-derivatives-minimizers
entries.title: Second derivatives for minimizers
entries.body:
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(i)] Let $u \in H^1(U)$ be a weak solution of the nonlinear partial differential equation (3), where $L$ satisfies (5), (6). Then
\[
u \in H^2_{\mathrm{loc}}(U).
\]
\item[(ii)] If in addition $u \in H_0^1(U)$ and $\partial U$ is $C^2$, then
\[
u \in H^2(U),
\]
with the estimate
\[
\|u\|_{H^2(U)} \le C\|f\|_{L^2(U)}.
\]
\end{enumerate}

entries.label: def:admissible-class-nonlinear-eigenvalue
entries.title: Admissible class for a nonlinear eigenvalue problem
entries.body:
Let us introduce the appropriate admissible class
\[
\mathcal{A} := \{w \in H_0^1(U) \mid J[w] = 0\}.
\]
We suppose also that the open set $U$ is bounded, connected and has a smooth boundary.

entries.label: thm:existence-constrained-minimizer-nonlinear-eigenvalue
entries.title: Existence of constrained minimizer
entries.body:
Assume the admissible set $\mathcal{A}$ is nonempty. Then there exists $u \in \mathcal{A}$ satisfying
\[
I[u] = \min_{w \in \mathcal{A}} I[w].
\]

deps.title: Admissible class for a nonlinear eigenvalue problem
deps.label: def:admissible-class-nonlinear-eigenvalue

entries.label: thm:lagrange-multiplier-nonlinear-eigenvalue
entries.title: Lagrange multiplier
entries.body:
Let $u \in \mathcal{A}$ satisfy
\[
I[u] = \min_{w \in \mathcal{A}} I[w].
\tag{9}
\]
Then there exists a real number $\lambda$ such that
\[
\int_U Du \cdot Dv\, dx = \lambda \int_U g(u)v\, dx
\tag{10}
\]
for all $v \in H_0^1(U)$.

entries.label: rem:nonlinear-eigenvalue-lagrange-multiplier-interpretation
entries.title: Nonlinear eigenvalue problem interpretation

entries.label: def:admissible-class-obstacle-problem
entries.title: Admissible class for the obstacle problem
entries.body: The set $\mathcal{A}$ of (25) is the admissible class for the obstacle problem with obstacle $h$.

entries.label: thm:existence-minimizer-obstacle-problem
entries.title: Existence of minimizer, obstacle problem
entries.body:
Assume the admissible set $\mathcal{A}$ is nonempty. Then there exists a unique function $u \in \mathcal{A}$ satisfying
\[
I[u] = \min_{w \in \mathcal{A}} I[w].
\]

deps.title: Admissible class for the obstacle problem
deps.label: def:admissible-class-obstacle-problem

entries.label: thm:variational-inequality-characterization
entries.title: Variational characterization of minimizer
entries.body:
Let $u \in \mathcal{A}$ be the unique solution of
\[
I[u] = \min_{w \in \mathcal{A}} I[w].
\]
Then
\[
\int_U Du \cdot D(w - u)\, dx \geq \int_U f(w - u)\, dx \quad \text{for all } w \in \mathcal{A}.
\tag{26}
\]
We call (26) a \textit{variational inequality}.

entries.label: rem:obstacle-problem-free-boundary-interpretation
entries.title: Interpretation of the variational inequality
entries.body:
To gain some insight into the variational inequality (26), let us quote without proof a regularity assertion (see Kinderlehrer--Stampacchia \cite{KinderlehrerStampacchia1980}), which states $u \in W^{2,\infty}(U)$, provided $\partial U$ is smooth. Hence the set
\[
O := \{x \in U \mid u(x) > h(x)\}
\]
is open, and
\[
C := \{x \in U \mid u(x) = h(x)\}
\]
is (relatively) closed.

\begin{figure}[h]\centering

\end{figure}
\noindent\textbf{The free boundary for the obstacle problem}

We claim that in fact $u \in C^\infty(O)$ and
\[
-\Delta u = f \quad \text{in } O.
\tag{28}
\]
To see this, fix any test function $v \in C_c^\infty(O)$. Then if $|\tau|$ is sufficiently small, $w := u + \tau v \geq h$, and so $w \in \mathcal{A}$. Thus (26) implies $\tau \int_O Du \cdot Dv - fv\, dx \geq 0$. This inequality is valid for all sufficiently small $\tau$, both positive and negative, and so in fact
\[
\int_O Du \cdot Dv - fv\, dx = 0
\]
for all $v \in C_c^\infty(O)$. Hence $u$ is a weak solution of (28); whence linear regularity theory (\cref{sec:elliptic-equations-definitions}) shows $u \in C^\infty(O)$.

Now if $v \in C_c^\infty(U)$ satisfies $v \geq 0$ and if $0 < \tau < 1$, then $w := u+\tau v \in \mathcal{A}$, whence $\int_U Du \cdot Dv - fv\, dx \geq 0$. But since $u \in W^{2,\infty}(U)$, we can integrate by parts to deduce $\int_U (-\Delta u - f)v\, dx \geq 0$ for all nonnegative functions $v \in C_c^\infty(U)$. Thus
\[
-\Delta u \geq f \quad \text{a.e. in } U.
\tag{29}
\]
We summarize our conclusions by observing from (28), (29) that
\[
\begin{cases} u \geq h, -\Delta u \geq f & \text{a.e. in } U \\ -\Delta u = f & \text{on } U \cap \{u > h\}. \end{cases}
\tag{30}
\]

The set
\[
F := \partial O \cap U
\]
is called the \textit{free boundary}. Many interesting problems in applied mathematics involve partial differential equations with free boundaries. Such of these problems as can be recast as variational inequalities become relatively easy to study, especially in that there is no explicit mention of the free boundary in the inequalities (30). Applications arise in stopping time optimal control problems for Brownian motion, in groundwater hydrology, in plasticity theory, etc. See Kinderlehrer--Stampacchia \cite{KinderlehrerStampacchia1980}.

entries.label: thm:euler-lagrange-harmonic-maps
entries.title: Euler--Lagrange equation for harmonic maps
entries.body:
Let $\mathbf{u} \in \mathcal{A}$ satisfy
\[
I[\mathbf{u}] = \min_{\mathbf{w} \in \mathcal{A}} I[\mathbf{w}].
\]
Then
\[
\int_U D\mathbf{u} : D\mathbf{v}\, dx = \int_U |D\mathbf{u}|^2 \mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v}\, dx
\tag{33}
\]
for each $\mathbf{v} \in H_0^1(U;\R^m) \cap L^\infty(U;\R^m)$.

entries.label: rem:harmonic-maps-lagrange-multiplier-function
entries.title: Harmonic map equation and the Lagrange multiplier function

entries.label: def:admissible-class-stokes-problem
entries.title: Admissible class for Stokes' problem
entries.body: The set $\mathcal{A}$ above, of divergence-free vector fields in $H_0^1(U;\R^3)$, is the admissible class for Stokes' problem.

entries.label: thm:pressure-lagrange-multiplier-stokes
entries.title: Pressure as Lagrange multiplier
entries.body:
There exists a scalar function $p \in L^2_{\text{loc}}(U)$ such that
\[
\int_U Du : Dv\, dx = \int_U p\, \Div \mathbf{v} + \mathbf{f}\cdot\mathbf{v}\, dx
\tag{39}
\]
for all $\mathbf{v} \in H^1(U;\R^3)$ with compact support within $U$.

entries.label: rem:stokes-problem-weak-formulation
entries.title: Weak formulation of Stokes' problem

entries.label: def:admissible-class-incompressible-elasticity
entries.title: Admissible class, incompressible elasticity
entries.body:
\[
\mathcal{A} := \{\mathbf{w} \in W^{1,q}(U; \R^3) \mid \mathbf{w} = \mathbf{g} \text{ on } \partial U,\ \det D\mathbf{w} = 1 \text{ a.e.}\}
\]
for some $q > 3$.

entries.label: thm:existence-minimizer-determinant-constraint
entries.title: Minimizers with determinant constraint
entries.body:
Assume the mapping
\[
P \mapsto L(P,x)
\]
is convex, and $L$ satisfies the coercivity condition
\[
L(P,x) \geq \alpha|P|^q - \beta \quad (P \in \mathbb{M}^{3 \times 3}, x \in U)
\]
for some $\alpha > 0$, $\beta \geq 0$. Suppose finally $\mathcal{A} \neq \emptyset$.

Then there exists $\mathbf{u} \in \mathcal{A}$ satisfying
\[
I[\mathbf{u}] = \min_{\mathbf{w} \in \mathcal{A}} I[\mathbf{w}].
\]

entries.label: rem:incompressibility-euler-formula
entries.title: Velocity versus displacement incompressibility conditions
entries.body:
It may seem odd that the incompressibility condition in the Stokes problem is
\[
\Div \mathbf{u} = 0
\tag{52}
\]
and in the nonlinear elasticity problem is
\[
\det D\mathbf{u} = 1.
\tag{53}
\]
The explanation is that $\mathbf{u}$ represents a velocity in (52) and a displacement in (53). More generally if $\mathbf{w}$ is a velocity field, say of a fluid, we compute the motion of a particle initially at a point $x$ by solving the ODE
\[
\begin{cases}
\dot{\mathbf{y}}(t) = \mathbf{w}(\mathbf{y}(t), t) & (t \in \R) \\
\mathbf{y}(0) = x.
\end{cases}
\]
Write $\mathbf{y}(t) = \mathbf{y}(t, x)$ to display the dependence on the initial position $x$. Then for each $t > 0$, the mapping $x \mapsto \mathbf{y}(t, x)$ is volume-preserving if
\[
J(x, t) = \det D_x \mathbf{y}(t, x) = 1 \quad \text{for all } x.
\]
Clearly $J(0, x) = 1$, and a calculation verifies \textit{Euler's formula}:
\[
J_t(x, t) = (\Div \mathbf{w})(\mathbf{y}(t, x), t)J(x, t),
\]
the divergence taken with respect to the spatial variables. Hence if $\Div \mathbf{w} = 0$, the flow is volume preserving.

entries.label: def:frechet-derivative-hilbert-space
entries.title: Fr\'echet differentiability
entries.body:
We say $I$ is differentiable at $u \in H$ if there exists $v \in H$ such that
\[
I[w] = I[u] + (v, w - u) + o(\|w - u\|) \quad (w \in H).
\tag{1}
\]
The element $v$, if it exists, is unique. We then write $I'[u] = v$.

entries.label: def:c1-functional-class-hilbert
entries.title: The class $C^1(H;\R)$
entries.body: We say $I$ belongs to $C^1(H;\R)$ if $I'[u]$ exists for each $u \in H$, and the mapping $I' : H \to H$ is continuous.

entries.label: rem:lipschitz-derivative-simplification
entries.title: Simplifying Lipschitz hypothesis
entries.body:
The theory we will develop below holds if $I \in C^1(H;\R)$, but the proofs will be greatly streamlined provided we additionally assume
\[
I' : H \to H \text{ is Lipschitz continuous on bounded subsets of } H.
\tag{2}
\]

entries.label: def:critical-point-critical-value
entries.title: Critical points and critical values
entries.body:
(i) We say $u \in H$ is a \textit{critical point} if $I'[u] = 0$.

(ii) The real number $c$ is a \textit{critical value} if $K_c \neq \emptyset$.

entries.label: def:palais-smale-condition
entries.title: Palais--Smale condition
entries.body:
A functional $I \in C^1(H;\R)$ satisfies the \textit{Palais--Smale compactness condition} if each sequence $\{u_k\}_{k=1}^\infty \subset H$ such that

(i) $\{I[u_k]\}_{k=1}^\infty$ is bounded

and

(ii) $I'[u_k] \to 0$ in $H$,

is precompact in $H$.

entries.label: thm:deformation-theorem
entries.title: Deformation Theorem
entries.body:
Assume $I \in \mathcal{C}$ satisfies the Palais--Smale condition. Suppose also
\[
K_c = \emptyset.
\tag{3}
\]
Then for each sufficiently small $\epsilon > 0$, there exists a constant $0 < \delta < \epsilon$ and a function
\[
\eta \in C([0,1] \times H; H)
\]
such that the mappings
\[
\eta_t(u) = \eta(t, u) \quad (0 \le t \le 1, u \in H)
\]
satisfy
\begin{itemize}
\item[(i)] $\eta_0(u) = u$ \quad $(u \in H)$,
\item[(ii)] $\eta_1(u) = u$ \quad $(u \notin I^{-1}[c - \epsilon, c + \epsilon])$,
\item[(iii)] $I[\eta_t(u)] \le I[u]$ \quad $(u \in H, 0 \le t \le 1)$,
\item[(iv)] $\eta_1(A_{c+\delta}) \subset A_{c-\delta}$.
\end{itemize}

entries.label: thm:mountain-pass-theorem
entries.title: Mountain Pass Theorem
entries.body:
Assume $I \in \mathcal{C}$ satisfies the Palais--Smale condition. Suppose also
\begin{itemize}
\item[(i)] $I[0] = 0$,
\item[(ii)] there exist constants $r, a > 0$ such that
\[
I[u] \geq a \text{ if } \|u\| = r,
\]
\item[(iii)] there exists an element $v \in H$ with
\[
\|v\| > r, \quad I[v] \leq 0.
\]
\end{itemize}
Define
\[
\Gamma := \{\mathbf{g} \in C([0,1]; H) \mid \mathbf{g}(0) = 0, \, \mathbf{g}(1) = v\}.
\]
Then
\[
c = \inf_{\mathbf{g} \in \Gamma} \max_{0 \le t \le 1} I[\mathbf{g}(t)]
\]
is a \textit{critical value} of $I$.

entries.label: rem:power-nonlinearity-semilinear-example
entries.title: A power-law example
entries.body:
Observe that the PDE
\[
-\Delta u = |u|^{p-1}u
\]
falls under the hypotheses above. We will return to this particular nonlinearity again in \S9.4.2.

entries.label: thm:existence-nontrivial-solution-semilinear-elliptic
entries.title: Existence of a nontrivial solution
entries.body: The boundary-value problem (22) has at least one weak solution $u \not\equiv 0$.

entries.label: def:monotone-vector-field
entries.title: Monotone vector field
entries.body:
A \textit{vector field} $\mathbf{a} : \R^n \to \R^n$ is called \textit{monotone} provided
\[
(\mathbf{a}(p) - \mathbf{a}(q)) \cdot (p - q) \geq 0
\tag{3}
\]
for all $p, q \in \R^n$.

entries.label: lem:zeros-of-a-vector-field
entries.title: Zeros of a vector field
entries.body:
Assume the continuous function $\mathbf{v} : \R^n \to \R^n$ satisfies
\[
\mathbf{v}(x) \cdot x \geq 0 \quad \text{if } |x| = r,
\tag{8}
\]
for some $r > 0$. Then there exists a point $x \in B(0,r)$ such that
\[
\mathbf{v}(x) = 0.
\]

entries.label: thm:monotonicity-approximate-solutions
entries.title: Construction of approximate solutions
entries.body: For each integer $m = 1, \ldots$, there exists a function $u_m$ of the form (6) satisfying the identities (7).

entries.label: thm:monotonicity-energy-estimates
entries.title: Energy estimates
entries.body:
There exists a constant $C$, depending only on $U$ and $\mathbf{a}$, such that
\[
\|u_m\|_{H^1_0(U)} \leq C(1 + \|f\|_{L^2(U)})
\tag{11}
\]
for $m = 1, 2, \ldots$.

entries.label: thm:monotonicity-existence-weak-solution
entries.title: Existence of weak solution
entries.body: There exists a weak solution of the nonlinear boundary-value problem (1).

entries.label: rem:browder-minty-method
entries.title: Method of Browder and Minty
entries.body: This use of monotonicity is the \textit{method of Browder and Minty}, a remarkable technique which somehow employs the inequality condition of monotonicity to justify passing to weak limits within a nonlinearity.

entries.label: thm:monotonicity-uniqueness-weak-solution
entries.title: Uniqueness of weak solution
entries.body: Assume the strict monotonicity property (20) holds. Then there exists only one weak solution of (1).

entries.label: rem:monotonicity-h2-regularity
entries.title: $H^2$ regularity under strict monotonicity
entries.body:
Under the strengthened monotonicity assumption (20) our weak solution $u$ in fact belongs to $H^2(U)$, and so satisfies
\[
-\Div\,\mathbf{a}(Du) = f \quad \text{a.e. in } U.
\]
To demonstrate this, we select $q, \xi \in \R^n$ and set $p = q + h\xi$, $h \neq 0$, in (20). We obtain, after dividing by $h^2$, the inequality
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{a^i(q + h\xi) - a^i(q)}{h} \xi_i \geq \theta|\xi|^2.
\]
Now send $h \to 0$:
\[
\sum_{i,j=1}^{n} a^i_{p_j}(q)\xi_i\xi_j \geq \theta|\xi|^2 \quad (q, \xi \in \R^n).
\tag{21}
\]
We can thus interpret the nonlinear PDE $-\Div\,\mathbf{a}(Du) = f$ as being uniformly elliptic. The proof of $H^2$ regularity of the weak solution now follows almost precisely as in the proof of Theorem 1 in \cref{thm:interior-h2-regularity}.

entries.label: thm:banach-fixed-point-theorem
entries.title: Banach's Fixed Point Theorem
entries.body:
Assume
\[
A : X \to X
\]
is a nonlinear mapping, and suppose that
\[
\|A[u] - A[\bar{u}]\| \leq \gamma \|u - \bar{u}\| \quad (u, \bar{u} \in X)
\tag{1}
\]
for some constant $\gamma < 1$. Then $A$ has a unique fixed point.

entries.label: def:strict-contraction
entries.title: Strict contraction
entries.body: We say that $A$ is a strict contraction if (1) holds.

entries.label: ex:reaction-diffusion-banach-fixed-point
entries.title: Reaction-diffusion equations

entries.label: def:weak-solution-reaction-diffusion-system
entries.title: Weak solution of the reaction-diffusion system
entries.body: A function $\mathbf{u}$ satisfying (5) is a \textit{weak solution} of (2) provided (6) and (7) hold.

entries.label: thm:reaction-diffusion-banach-existence
entries.title: Existence for the reaction-diffusion system
entries.body: There exists a unique weak solution of (2).

deps.title: Weak solution of the reaction-diffusion system
deps.label: def:weak-solution-reaction-diffusion-system

entries.label: rem:reaction-diffusion-chemical-interpretation
entries.title: Chemical interpretation of the reaction-diffusion system
entries.body: In common applications problem (2) records the evolution of the densities $u^1, \ldots, u^m$ of various chemicals, which both diffuse within a medium and interact with each other. The diffusion term is $\Delta \mathbf{u}$ (or more generally $(a_1 \Delta u^1, \ldots, a_m \Delta u^m)$ where the constants $a_k > 0$ characterize the diffusion of the $k^{\text{th}}$ chemical). The reaction term $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{u})$ models the chemistry. In the foregoing example we made the unreasonable assumption that $\mathbf{f}$ is globally Lipschitz. In more realistic models $\mathbf{f}$ is often a polynomial in $\mathbf{u}$ and there are interesting problems as to the global existence or blow-up of a solution. (A simple such problem is treated in \cref{sec:blow-up}.)

entries.label: thm:schauders-fixed-point-theorem
entries.title: Schauder's Fixed Point Theorem
entries.body:
Suppose $K \subset X$ is compact and convex, and assume also
\[
A : K \to K
\]
is continuous. Then $A$ has a fixed point in $K$.

entries.label: def:compact-mapping-banach-space
entries.title: Compact mapping
entries.body: A nonlinear mapping $A : X \to X$ is called \textit{compact} provided for each bounded sequence $\{u_k\}_{k=1}^{\infty}$ the sequence $\{A[u_k]\}_{k=1}^{\infty}$ is precompact; that is, there exists a subsequence $\{u_{k_j}\}_{j=1}^{\infty}$ such that $\{A[u_{k_j}]\}_{j=1}^{\infty}$ converges in $X$.

entries.label: thm:schaefers-fixed-point-theorem
entries.title: Schaefer's Fixed Point Theorem
entries.body:
Suppose
\[
A : X \to X
\]
is a continuous and compact mapping. Assume further that the set
\[
\{u \in X \mid u = \lambda A[u] \text{ for some } 0 \le \lambda \le 1\}
\]
is bounded. Then $A$ has a fixed point.

entries.label: rem:a-priori-estimates-method
entries.title: The method of a priori estimates
entries.body: The assertion is that if we have a bound on any possible fixed points of any of the operators $\lambda A$ for $0 \le \lambda \le 1$, then we have the existence of a fixed point for $A$. This is in accordance with the remarkable informal principle that ``if we can prove appropriate estimates for solutions of a nonlinear PDE, under the assumption that such solutions exist, then in fact these solutions do exist''. This is the method of \textit{a priori}\footnote{\textit{a priori} = from before (Latin).} estimates.

entries.label: ex:quasilinear-elliptic-schaefer
entries.title: A quasilinear elliptic PDE
entries.body:
We present now a simple application of Schaefer's theorem by solving the semilinear boundary-value problem
\[
\begin{cases}
-\Delta u + b(Du) + \mu u = 0 & \text{in } U \\
u = 0 & \text{on } \partial U,
\end{cases}
\tag{21}
\]
where $U$ is bounded and $\partial U$ is smooth. We assume $b : \R^n \to \R$ is smooth, Lipschitz continuous, and thus satisfies the growth condition
\[
|b(p)| \le C(|p| + 1)
\tag{22}
\]
for some constant $C$ and all $p \in \R^n$.

entries.label: thm:schaefer-quasilinear-existence
entries.title: Existence for the quasilinear elliptic PDE
entries.body: If $\mu > 0$ is sufficiently large, there exists a function $u \in H^2(U) \cap H^1_0(U)$ solving the boundary-value problem (21).

entries.label: rem:iteration-scheme-warning
entries.title: Remark and warning
entries.body:
A plausible plan for constructively solving (21) would be to select some $u^0$ and then iteratively solve the linear boundary-value problems
\[
\begin{cases}
-\Delta u^{k+1} + \mu u^{k+1} = -b(Du^k) & \text{in } U \\
u^{k+1} = 0 & \text{on } \partial U
\end{cases} \quad (k = 0, 1, \ldots).
\]
However, we \textit{cannot} assert that $\{u^k\}_{k=0}^\infty$ then converges to a solution of (21). Schauder's and Schaefer's fixed point theorems do not say that any sequence converges to a fixed point. (But see the proof in \cref{sec:subsolutions-supersolutions} following.)

deps.title: Existence for the quasilinear elliptic PDE
deps.label: thm:schaefer-quasilinear-existence

entries.label: def:weak-sub-supersolution
entries.title: Weak sub- and supersolutions
entries.body:
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(i)] We say that $\bar{u} \in H^1(U)$ is a \textit{weak supersolution} of problem (1) if
\[
\int_U D\bar{u} \cdot Dv \, dx \geq \int_U f(\bar{u})v \, dx
\tag{3}
\]
for each $v \in H^1_0(U), v \geq 0$ a.e.
\item[(ii)] Similarly, $\underline{u} \in H^1(U)$ is a \textit{weak subsolution} provided
\[
\int_U D\underline{u} \cdot Dv \, dx \leq \int_U f(\underline{u})v \, dx
\tag{4}
\]
for each $v \in H^1_0(U), v \geq 0$ a.e.
\item[(iii)] We say $u \in H^1_0(U)$ is a \textit{weak solution} of (1) if
\[
\int_U Du \cdot Dv \, dx = \int_U f(u)v \, dx
\]
for each $v \in H^1_0(U)$.
\end{enumerate}

entries.label: rem:sub-supersolution-c2-case
entries.title: Classical interpretation
entries.body:
If $\bar{u}, \underline{u} \in C^2(U)$, then from (3) and (4) it follows that
\[
-\Delta \bar{u} \geq f(\bar{u}), \quad -\Delta \underline{u} \leq f(\underline{u}) \quad \text{in } U.
\]

entries.label: thm:existence-between-sub-supersolutions
entries.title: Existence of a solution between sub- and supersolutions
entries.body:
Assume there exist a weak supersolution $\bar{u}$ and a weak subsolution $\underline{u}$ of (1), satisfying
\[
\underline{u} \leq 0, \quad \bar{u} \geq 0 \quad \text{on } \partial U \text{ in the trace sense}, \quad \underline{u} \leq \bar{u} \text{ a.e. in } U.
\tag{5}
\]
Then there exists a weak solution $u$ of (1), such that
\[
\underline{u} \leq u \leq \bar{u} \quad \text{a.e. in } U.
\]

deps.title: First Existence Theorem for weak solutions
deps.label: thm:first-existence-weak-solutions

entries.label: thm:blowup-large-data
entries.title: Blow-up for large initial data
entries.body:
Let $u$ be a smooth solution of (1) with $g \geq 0$, $g \not\equiv 0$, and set $w_1$, $\eta(t)$ as above. If
\[
\eta(0) = \int_U gw_1 \, dx > \lambda_1,
\]
then $u$ cannot exist as a smooth solution on all of $[0,T]$ for $T$ large enough; more precisely, either the solution fails to be smooth enough to justify the calculation below, or else
\[
\lim_{t \to t_*} \int_U u(x,t)w_1(x) \, dx = \infty
\]
for some $0 < t_* \le t^*$, where $t^*$ is as in (10) below. In this case we say $u$ ``blows up'' at time $t_*$.

entries.label: def:star-shaped-domain
entries.title: Star-shaped domain
entries.body:
An open set $U$ is called \textit{star-shaped with respect to} $0$ provided for each $x \in \overline{U}$, the line segment
\[
\{\lambda x \mid 0 \leq \lambda \leq 1\}
\]
lies in $\overline{U}$.

entries.label: lem:normals-star-shaped-region
entries.title: Normals to a star-shaped region
entries.body:
Assume $\partial U$ is $C^1$ and $U$ is star-shaped with respect to 0. Then
\[
x \cdot \nu(x) \geq 0 \quad \text{for all } x \in \partial U,
\]
where $\nu$ denotes the unit outward normal.

entries.label: thm:derrick-pohozaev-nonexistence
entries.title: Nonexistence of nontrivial solution
entries.body:
Assume $u \in C^2(\overline{U})$ is a solution of problem (11), and the exponent $p$ satisfies inequality (13). Suppose further $U$ is star-shaped with respect to 0, and $\partial U$ is $C^1$. Then
\[
u \equiv 0 \quad \text{within } U.
\]

entries.label: rem:derrick-pohozaev-identity-name
entries.title: Derrick--Pohozaev identity
entries.body: Equality (18) is sometimes called the \textit{Derrick--Pohozaev identity}.

entries.label: thm:star-shaped-level-sets
entries.title: Star-shaped level sets
entries.body:
For each $0 < \lambda < 1$ the level set
\[
\Gamma_\lambda := \{x \in U \mid u(x) = \lambda\}
\]
is a smooth surface and is the boundary of a set star-shaped with respect to $0$.

entries.label: lem:hopf-lemma-refinement
entries.title: A refinement of Hopf's Lemma

entries.label: lem:boundary-estimates-radial-symmetry
entries.title: Boundary estimates
entries.body:
Let $u \in C^2(\overline{U})$ satisfy (2), (3). Then for each point $x^0 \in \partial U \cap \{x_n > 0\}$, either
\[
u_{x_n}(x^0) < 0
\tag{7}
\]
or else
\[
u_{x_n}(x^0) = 0, \quad u_{x_nx_n}(x^0) > 0.
\tag{8}
\]
In either case, $u$ is strictly decreasing as a function of $x_n$ near $x^0$.

entries.label: def:moving-plane-notation
entries.title: Moving plane notation
entries.body:
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(i)] If $0 \le \lambda \le 1$, define the plane
\[
P_\lambda := \{x \in \R^n \mid x_n = \lambda\}.
\]
\item[(ii)] Write $x_\lambda := (x_1, \ldots, x_{n-1}, 2\lambda - x_n)$ to denote the \textit{reflection} of $x$ in $P_\lambda$.
\item[(iii)] $E_\lambda := \{x \in U \mid \lambda < x_n < 1\}$.
\end{enumerate}

entries.label: thm:radial-symmetry-positive-solutions
entries.title: Radial symmetry
entries.body:
Let $u \in C^2(\overline{U})$ solve (2), (3). Then $u$ is radial; that is,
\[
u(x) = v(r) \quad (r = |x|)
\]
for some strictly decreasing function $v : [0, 1] \to [0, \infty)$.

entries.label: def:convex-function-hilbert-space
entries.title: Convex function on a Hilbert space
entries.body:
A function
\[
I : H \to (-\infty, \infty]
\]
is \textit{convex} provided
\[
I[\tau u + (1-\tau)v] \le \tau I[u] + (1-\tau)I[v]
\]
for all $u, v \in H$ and each $0 \le \tau \le 1$.

entries.label: def:lower-semicontinuous-hilbert-functional
entries.title: Lower semicontinuous
entries.body:
We say $I : H \to (-\infty, +\infty]$ is \textit{lower semicontinuous} if
\[
\begin{cases} u_k \to u \text{ in } H \text{ implies} \\ I[u] \le \liminf_{k \to \infty} I[u_k]. \end{cases}
\]

entries.label: def:subdifferential-hilbert
entries.title: Subdifferential

deps.title: Convex function on a Hilbert space
deps.label: def:convex-function-hilbert-space

entries.label: thm:properties-of-subdifferentials
entries.title: Properties of subdifferentials
entries.body:
Let $I : H \to (-\infty, +\infty]$ be convex, proper and lower semicontinuous. Then
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(i)] $D(\partial I) \subseteq D(I)$.
\item[(ii)] If $v \in \partial I[u]$ and $\tilde{v} \in \partial I[\tilde{u}]$, then
\[
(v - \tilde{v}, u - \tilde{u}) \geq 0 \quad \text{(monotonicity)}.
\]
\item[(iii)] $I[u] = \min_{w \in H} I[w]$ if and only if $0 \in \partial I[u]$.
\item[(iv)] For each $w \in H$ and $\lambda > 0$, the problem
\[
u + \lambda \partial I[u] \ni w
\]
has a unique solution $u \in D(\partial I)$.
\end{enumerate}
Assertion (iv) means that there exists $u \in D(\partial I)$ and $v \in \partial I[u]$ such that
\[
u + \lambda v = w.
\]

entries.label: def:nonlinear-resolvent-yosida
entries.title: Nonlinear resolvent and Yosida approximation
entries.body:
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(1)] For each $\lambda > 0$ define the \textit{nonlinear resolvent} $J_\lambda : H \to D(\partial I)$ by setting
\[
J_\lambda[w] := u,
\]
where $u$ is the unique solution of
\[
u + \lambda \partial I[u] \ni w.
\]
\item[(2)] For each $\lambda > 0$ define the \textit{Yosida approximation} $A_\lambda : H \to H$ by
\[
A_\lambda[w] := \frac{w - J_\lambda[w]}{\lambda} \quad (w \in H).
\tag{7}
\]
\end{enumerate}
Think of $A_\lambda$ as a sort of regularization or smoothing of the operator $A = \partial I$.

entries.label: thm:properties-resolvent-yosida
entries.title: Properties of $J_\lambda$, $A_\lambda$
entries.body:
For each $\lambda > 0$ and $w, \tilde{w} \in H$, the following statements hold:
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(i)] $\|J_\lambda[w] - J_\lambda[\tilde{w}]\| \le \|w - \tilde{w}\|$,
\item[(ii)] $\|A_\lambda[w] - A_\lambda[\tilde{w}]\| \le \frac{2}{\lambda}\|w - \tilde{w}\|$,
\item[(iii)] $0 \le (w - \tilde{w}, A_\lambda[w] - A_\lambda[\tilde{w}])$,
\item[(iv)] $A_\lambda[w] \in \partial I[J_\lambda[w]]$.
\item[(v)] If $w \in D(\partial I)$, then
\[
\sup_{\lambda > 0} \|A_\lambda[w]\| \le |A^0[w]|,
\]
where $|A^0[w]| := \min_{z \in \partial I[w]} \|z\|$.
\item[(vi)] For each $w \in \overline{D(\partial I)}$,
\[
\lim_{\lambda \to 0} J_\lambda[w] = w.
\]
\end{enumerate}

entries.label: rem:nonlinear-semigroup-notation
entries.title: Nonlinear semigroup notation
entries.body: We will employ the notation (10) to emphasize similarities with linear semigroup theory, previously introduced in \S7.4. But carefully note here and afterwards that the mapping $u \mapsto S(t)u$ is in general \textit{nonlinear}.

entries.label: def:nonlinear-contraction-semigroup
entries.title: Nonlinear (contraction) semigroup

entries.label: thm:solution-of-gradient-flow
entries.title: Solution of gradient flow

entries.label: rem:gradient-flow-semigroup-extension
entries.title: Extension to $\overline{D(\partial I)}$
entries.body:
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(i)] The operator $A = \partial I$ in fact generates a nonlinear contraction semigroup on all of $H$. If $\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \in D(\partial I)$, we write as above
\[
\lim_{\lambda \to 0} \mathbf{u}_\lambda(t) = \mathbf{u}(t) = S(t)\mathbf{u}
\]
and
\[
\lim_{\lambda \to 0} \mathbf{v}_\lambda(t) = \mathbf{v}(t) = S(t)\mathbf{v}.
\]
Owing to (19) we see
\[
\|S(t)\mathbf{u} - S(t)\mathbf{v}\| \leq \|\mathbf{u} - \mathbf{v}\| \quad (t \geq 0)
\]
if $\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \in D(\partial I)$. Using this inequality we uniquely extend the semigroup of nonlinear operators $\{S(t)\}_{t \geq 0}$ to $H = \overline{D(\partial I)}$.
\item[(ii)] We have assumed that $D(\partial I)$ is dense in $H$ purely to streamline the exposition: in general $\{S(t)\}_{t \geq 0}$ is a semigroup of contractions on $\overline{D(\partial I)} \subseteq H$.
\end{enumerate}

entries.label: thm:characterization-subdifferential-uniformly-convex-lagrangian
entries.title: Characterization of $\partial I$

deps.title: Convex function on a Hilbert space
deps.label: def:convex-function-hilbert-space

entries.label: ex:quasilinear-parabolic-gradient-flow
entries.title: Quasilinear parabolic PDE via gradient flows
entries.body:
We can now look at the initial/boundary-value problem:
\[
\begin{cases}
u_t - \sum_{i=1}^n (L_{p_i}(Du))_{x_i} = 0 & \text{in } U \times (0, \infty) \\
u = 0 & \text{on } \partial U \times (0, \infty) \\
u = g & \text{on } U \times \{t = 0\},
\end{cases}
\tag{33}
\]
where $g \in L^2(U)$. In accordance with Theorem 4, we can recast this problem into the abstract form
\[
\begin{cases} u'(t) = -\partial I[u(t)] & (t \geq 0) \\ u(0) = g. \end{cases}
\tag{34}
\]
We apply Theorem 3. If $g \in H^2(U) \cap H^1_0(U)$, there exists a unique function
\[
u \in C([0,\infty); L^2(U)), \text{ with } u' \in L^\infty((0,\infty); L^2(U)),
\]
that is, a weak solution of (33). In view of the estimate
\[
\|u(t)\|_{H^2(U)} \leq C\|u'(t)\|_{L^2(U)},
\]
we see $u \in L^\infty((0,\infty); H^2(U) \cap H^1_0(U))$ as well.

entries.label: rem:hj-viscosity-solution-motivation
entries.title: Motivation for definition of viscosity solution
entries.body:
The technique alluded to above works as follows. Fix any smooth test function $v \in C^\infty(\R^n \times (0,\infty))$ and suppose
\[
\begin{cases} u - v \text{ has a } \textit{strict local maximum} \text{ at some point} \\ (x_0, t_0) \in \R^n \times (0,\infty). \end{cases}
\tag{5}
\]
This means
\[
(u-v)(x_0, t_0) > (u-v)(x,t)
\]
for all points $(x,t)$ sufficiently close to $(x_0, t_0)$, with $(x,t) \neq (x_0, t_0)$.

Now recall (3). We claim for each sufficiently small $\epsilon_j > 0$, there exists a point $(x_{\epsilon_j}, t_{\epsilon_j})$ such that
\[
u^{\epsilon_j} - v \text{ has a local maximum at } (x_{\epsilon_j}, t_{\epsilon_j})
\tag{6}
\]
and
\[
(x_{\epsilon_j}, t_{\epsilon_j}) \to (x_0, t_0) \quad \text{as } j \to \infty.
\tag{7}
\]

To confirm this, note that for each sufficiently small $r > 0$, (5) implies $\max_{\overline{B}}(u-v) < (u-v)(x_0, t_0)$, $B$ denoting the closed ball in $\R^{n+1}$ with center $(x_0, t_0)$ and radius $r$. In view of (3), $u^{\epsilon_j} \to u$ uniformly on $B$, and so $\max_{\overline{B}}(u^{\epsilon_j}-v) < (u^{\epsilon_j}-v)(x_0, t_0)$ provided $\epsilon_j$ is small enough. Consequently $u^{\epsilon_j} - v$ attains a local maximum at some point in the interior of $B$. We can next replace $r$ by a sequence of radii tending to zero to obtain (6), (7).

Now owing to (6) we see that the equations
\[
Du^{\epsilon_j}(x_{\epsilon_j}, t_{\epsilon_j}) = Dv(x_{\epsilon_j}, t_{\epsilon_j}),
\tag{8}
\]
\[
u_t^{\epsilon_j}(x_{\epsilon_j}, t_{\epsilon_j}) = v_t(x_{\epsilon_j}, t_{\epsilon_j}),
\tag{9}
\]
and the inequality
\[
-\Delta u^{\epsilon_j}(x_{\epsilon_j}, t_{\epsilon_j}) \geq -\Delta v(x_{\epsilon_j}, t_{\epsilon_j})
\tag{10}
\]
hold. We consequently can calculate
\[
\begin{aligned}
v_t(x_\epsilon, t_\epsilon) + H(Dv(x_\epsilon, t_\epsilon), x_\epsilon)
&= u_t^\epsilon(x_\epsilon, t_\epsilon) + H(Du^\epsilon(x_\epsilon, t_\epsilon), x_\epsilon) && \text{by }(8),(9) \\
&= \epsilon \Delta u^\epsilon(x_\epsilon, t_\epsilon) && \text{by }(2) \\
&\leq \epsilon \Delta v(x_\epsilon, t_\epsilon) && \text{by }(10).
\end{aligned}
\tag{11}
\]

Now let $\epsilon \to 0$ and remember (7). Since $v$ is smooth and $H$ is continuous, we deduce
\[
v_t(x_0, t_0) + H(Dv(x_0, t_0), x_0) \leq 0.
\tag{12}
\]

We have established this inequality assuming (5). Suppose now instead that
\[
u - v \text{ has a local maximum at }(x_0, t_0),
\tag{13}
\]
but that this $\textit{maximum is not necessarily strict}$. Then $u - \bar{v}$ has a strict local maximum at $(x_0, t_0)$, for $\bar{v}(x, t) := v(x, t) + \delta(|x - x_0|^2 + (t - t_0)^2)$ ($\delta > 0$). We thus conclude as above that $\bar{v}_t(x_0, t_0) + H(D\bar{v}(x_0, t_0), x_0) \leq 0$; whereupon (12) again follows.

Consequently (13) implies inequality (12). Similarly, we deduce the reverse inequality
\[
v_t(x_0, t_0) + H(Dv(x_0, t_0), x_0) \geq 0,
\tag{14}
\]
provided
\[
u - v \text{ has a local minimum at }(x_0, t_0).
\tag{15}
\]
The proof is exactly like that above, except that the inequalities in (10), and thus in (11), are reversed.

In summary, we have discovered for any smooth function $v$ that inequality (12) follows from (13), and (14) from (15). We have in effect put the derivatives onto $v$, at the expense of certain inequalities holding. $\square$

entries.label: def:viscosity-solution-cauchy-problem
entries.title: Viscosity solution of the initial-value problem
entries.body:
A bounded, uniformly continuous function $u$ is called a \textit{viscosity solution} of the \textit{initial-value problem} (1) for the \textit{Hamilton-Jacobi equation} provided:

(i) $u = g$ on $\R^n \times \{t = 0\}$,

and

(ii) for each $v \in C^\infty(\R^n \times (0,\infty))$,

if $u - v$ has a local maximum at a point $(x_0, t_0) \in \R^n \times (0,\infty)$, then
\[
v_t(x_0, t_0) + H(Dv(x_0, t_0), x_0) \leq 0,
\tag{16}
\]
and

if $u - v$ has a local minimum at a point $(x_0, t_0) \in \R^n \times (0,\infty)$, then
\[
v_t(x_0, t_0) + H(Dv(x_0, t_0), x_0) \geq 0.
\tag{17}
\]

deps.title: Motivation for definition of viscosity solution
deps.label: rem:hj-viscosity-solution-motivation

entries.label: rem:viscosity-solution-definition-emphasis
entries.title: The defining inequalities, not the vanishing-viscosity construction, are the definition
entries.body:
Note carefully that by definition a viscosity solution satisfies (16), (17), and so all subsequent deductions must be based on these inequalities. The previous discussion was purely motivational.

For emphasis, we repeat the same point, which has caused some confusion among students. To verify that a given function $u$ is a viscosity solution of the Hamilton-Jacobi equation $u_t + H(Du, x) = 0$, we must confirm that (16), (17) hold for all smooth functions $v$. Now the argument above shows that \textit{if} $u$ is constructed using the vanishing viscosity method, it is indeed a viscosity solution. But we will also see later in \S10.3 that viscosity solutions can be built in entirely different ways, which have nothing whatsoever to do with vanishing viscosity.

The point is that the inequalities (16), (17) provide an intrinsic characterization, and indeed the very definition, of our generalized solutions. $\square$

deps.title: Viscosity solution of the initial-value problem
deps.label: def:viscosity-solution-cauchy-problem

entries.label: prop:classical-solutions-are-viscosity-solutions
entries.title: Classical solutions are viscosity solutions
entries.body: If $u \in C^1(\R^n \times [0,\infty))$ solves (1) and if $u$ is bounded and uniformly continuous, then $u$ is a viscosity solution. That is, any \textit{classical solution} of $u_t + H(Du,x) = 0$ \textit{is also a viscosity solution}.

deps.title: Viscosity solution of the initial-value problem
deps.label: def:viscosity-solution-cauchy-problem

entries.label: lem:touching-by-c1-function
entries.title: Touching by a $C^1$ function
entries.body:
Assume $u : \R^n \to \R$ is continuous and is also differentiable at some point $x_0$. Then there exists a function $v \in C^1(\R^n)$ such that
\[
u(x_0) = v(x_0)
\tag{18}
\]
and
\[
u - v \text{ has a strict local maximum at } x_0.
\tag{19}
\]

entries.label: thm:consistency-viscosity-solutions
entries.title: Consistency of viscosity solutions
entries.body:
Let $u$ be a viscosity solution of (1), and suppose $u$ is differentiable at some point $(x_0, t_0) \in \R^n \times (0, \infty)$. Then
\[
u_t(x_0, t_0) + H(Du(x_0, t_0), x_0) = 0.
\]

deps.title: Viscosity solution of the initial-value problem
deps.label: def:viscosity-solution-cauchy-problem

entries.label: def:viscosity-solution-finite-horizon
entries.title: Viscosity solution on a finite time interval
entries.body: We say that a bounded, uniformly continuous function $u$ is a viscosity solution of (1) provided $u = g$ on $\R^n \times \{t = 0\}$, and the inequalities in (16) (or (17)) from \S10.1 hold if $u - v$ has a local maximum (or minimum) at a point $(x_0, t_0) \in \R^n \times (0, T]$.

deps.title: Viscosity solution of the initial-value problem
deps.label: def:viscosity-solution-cauchy-problem

entries.label: lem:extrema-at-terminal-time
entries.title: Extrema at a terminal time
entries.body:
Assume $u$ is a viscosity solution of (1) and $u - v$ has a local maximum (minimum) at a point $(x_0, t_0) \in \R^n \times (0, T]$. Then
\[
v_t(x_0, t_0) + H(Dv(x_0, t_0), x_0) \le 0 \quad (\ge 0).
\tag{2}
\]
The point is that we are now allowing for $t_0 = T$.

deps.title: Viscosity solution on a finite time interval
deps.label: def:viscosity-solution-finite-horizon

entries.label: def:hamiltonian-lipschitz-condition
entries.title: Lipschitz hypothesis on the Hamiltonian

entries.label: thm:uniqueness-viscosity-solution-hj
entries.title: Uniqueness of viscosity solution
entries.body: Under assumption (3) there exists at most one viscosity solution of (1).

deps.title: Viscosity solution on a finite time interval
deps.label: def:viscosity-solution-finite-horizon

entries.label: rem:doubling-variables-technique
entries.title: Doubling of variables technique
entries.body: The following proof is based upon an unusual idea of ``doubling the number of variables''. See the proof of Theorem 3 in \S11.4.3 for a related technique. $\square$

entries.label: def:controlled-ode-admissible-controls
entries.title: Controlled ODE system and admissible controls
entries.body:
We will now study the possibility of optimally controlling the solution $\mathbf{x}(\cdot)$ of the ordinary differential equation
\[
\begin{cases} \dot{\mathbf{x}}(s) = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}(s), \alpha(s)) & (t < s < T) \\ \mathbf{x}(t) = x. \end{cases}
\tag{1}
\]
Here $\dot{} = \frac{d}{ds}$, $T > 0$ is a fixed terminal time, and $x \in \R^n$ is a given initial point, taken on by our solution $\mathbf{x}(\cdot)$ at the starting time $t \geq 0$. At later times $t < s < T$, $\mathbf{x}(\cdot)$ evolves according to the ODE, where
\[
\mathbf{f} : \R^n \times A \to \R^n
\]
is a given bounded, Lipschitz continuous function, and $A$ is some given compact subset of, say, $\R^m$. The function $\alpha(\cdot)$ appearing in (1) is a \textit{control}, that is, some appropriate scheme for adjusting parameters from the set $A$ as time evolves, thereby affecting the dynamics of the system modeled by (1).

Let us write
\[
\mathcal{A} = \{\alpha : [0,T] \to A \mid \alpha(\cdot) \text{ is measurable}\}
\tag{2}
\]
to denote the set of \textit{admissible controls}. Then since
\[
|f(x,a)| \leq C, \quad |f(x,a) - f(y,a)| \leq C|x - y| \quad (x,y \in \R^n, a \in A)
\tag{3}
\]
for some constant $C$, we see that for each control $\alpha(\cdot) \in \mathcal{A}$, the ODE (1) has a unique, Lipschitz continuous solution $\mathbf{x}(\cdot) = \mathbf{x}^{\alpha(\cdot)}(\cdot)$, existing on the time interval $[t, T]$ and solving the ODE for a.e. time $t < s < T$. We call $\mathbf{x}(\cdot)$ the \textit{response} of the system to the control $\alpha(\cdot)$, and $\mathbf{x}(s)$ the \textit{state} of the system at time $s$.

entries.label: def:control-cost-functional
entries.title: Running cost, terminal cost, and the cost functional
entries.body:
Given $x \in \R^n$ and $0 \le t \le T$, let us define for each admissible control $\alpha(\cdot) \in \mathcal{A}$ the corresponding cost
\[
C_{x,t}[\alpha(\cdot)] := \int_t^T h(x(s), \alpha(s)) \, ds + g(x(T)),
\tag{4}
\]
where $x(\cdot) = x^{\alpha(\cdot)}(\cdot)$ solves the ODE (1) and
\[
h : \R^n \times A \to \R, \quad g : \R^n \to \R
\]
are given functions. We call $h$ the \textit{running cost per unit time} and $g$ the \textit{terminal cost}, and will henceforth assume
\[
\begin{cases} |h(x, a)|, |g(x)| \le C \\ |h(x, a) - h(y, a)|, |g(x) - g(y)| \le C|x - y| \end{cases} \quad (x, y \in \R^n, a \in A)
\tag{5}
\]
for some constant $C$.

Given now $x \in \R^n$ and $0 \le t \le T$, we would like to find if possible a control $\alpha^*(\cdot)$ which minimizes the cost functional (4) among all other admissible controls.

deps.title: Controlled ODE system and admissible controls
deps.label: def:controlled-ode-admissible-controls

entries.label: def:value-function
entries.title: Value function

deps.title: Running cost, terminal cost, and the cost functional
deps.label: def:control-cost-functional

entries.label: thm:dynamic-programming-optimality-conditions
entries.title: Optimality conditions
entries.body:
For each $h > 0$ so small that $t + h \leq T$, we have
\[
u(x,t) = \inf_{\alpha(\cdot) \in \mathcal{A}} \left\{ \int_t^{t+h} h(\mathbf{x}(s), \alpha(s)) ds + u(\mathbf{x}(t+h), t+h) \right\},
\tag{7}
\]
where $\mathbf{x}(\cdot) = \mathbf{x}^{\alpha(\cdot)}(\cdot)$ solves the ODE (1) for the control $\alpha(\cdot)$.

deps.title: Running cost, terminal cost, and the cost functional
deps.label: def:control-cost-functional

entries.label: lem:value-function-estimates
entries.title: Estimates for the value function
entries.body:
There exists a constant $C$ such that
\[
|u(x,t)| \leq C,
\]
\[
|u(x,t) - u(\hat{x},\hat{t})| \leq C(|x - \hat{x}| + |t - \hat{t}|)
\]
for all $x, \hat{x} \in \R^n$, $0 \leq t, \hat{t} \leq T$.

deps.title: Running cost, terminal cost, and the cost functional
deps.label: def:control-cost-functional

entries.label: thm:value-function-solves-hjb-equation
entries.title: A PDE for the value function

entries.label: rem:hjb-hamiltonian-formula
entries.title: The Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman Hamiltonian
entries.body:
The Hamilton-Jacobi Bellman PDE has the form
\[
u_t + H(Du, x) = 0 \quad \text{in } \R^n \times (0,T),
\]
for the Hamiltonian
\[
H(p,x) := \min_{a \in A}\{f(x,a) \cdot p + h(x,a)\} \quad (p, x \in \R^n).
\tag{23}
\]
From the inequalities (5) in \S10.3, we deduce that $H$ satisfies the estimates (3) from \cref{def:hamiltonian-lipschitz-condition} in \S10.2. $\square$

deps.title: A PDE for the value function
deps.label: thm:value-function-solves-hjb-equation

entries.label: def:viscosity-solution-terminal-value-problem
entries.title: Viscosity solution of a terminal-value problem
entries.body:
Let us say that a bounded, uniformly continuous function $u$ is a \textit{viscosity solution} of (22) provided:

(i) $u = g$ on $\R^n \times \{t = T\}$,

and

(ii) for each $v \in C^\infty(\R^n \times (0,T))$

\[
\begin{cases}
\text{if } u - v \text{ has a local maximum at a point } (x_0, t_0) \in \R^n \times (0,T), \\
\text{then} \\
v_t(x_0, t_0) + H(Dv(x_0, t_0), x_0) \geq 0,
\end{cases}
\tag{24}
\]
and
\[
\begin{cases}
\text{if } u - v \text{ has a local minimum at a point } (x_0, t_0) \in \R^n \times (0,T), \\
\text{then} \\
v_t(x_0, t_0) + H(Dv(x_0, t_0), x_0) \leq 0.
\end{cases}
\tag{25}
\]

Observe that for our terminal-value problem (22) we \textit{reverse} the sense of the inequalities from those for the initial-value problem (\cref{def:viscosity-solution-cauchy-problem}).

deps.title: Viscosity solution of the initial-value problem
deps.label: def:viscosity-solution-cauchy-problem

entries.label: rem:terminal-value-time-reversal
entries.title: Correspondence with the initial-value problem via time reversal
entries.body:
The reader should check that if $u$ is the viscosity solution of (22), then $w(x,t) := u(x, T - t)$ ($x \in \R^n$, $0 \leq t \leq T$) is the viscosity solution of the initial-value problem
\[
\begin{cases}
w_t - H(Dw, x) = 0 & \text{in } \R^n \times (0,T) \\
w = g & \text{on } \R^n \times \{t = 0\}.
\end{cases}
\]

deps.label: def:viscosity-solution-terminal-value-problem
